coredata.dc:title: Wood-based carbon quantum dots for enhanced photocatalysis of MIL-88B(Fe)
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Huadong
Gong, Xinchao
Song, Zihui
Zhang, Shuo
Du, Wenxin
Nguyen, Tat Thang
Guo, Minghui
Gao, Xing
coredata.dc:description: To improve the Fenton-like photodegradation efficiency of MIL-88B(Fe) in visible light, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared from poplar wood powder as a raw material and added to a precursor solution of MIL-88B(Fe) to prepare CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) materials using hydrothermal method. CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Brunner Emmett Teller (BET), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Ultraviolet visible light spectrophotometry (UV–vis), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectra (PL), and photocurrent test. In this study, methylene blue (MB) was used as the target pollutant to test the catalytic performance of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), and the main free radicals involved in the reaction were determined by adding superoxide dismutase (SOD) and isopropanol (IPA) for comparative experiments. The experimental results show that because CQDs can be used as a photosensitizer and electron trapping agent, the light absorption capacity and photogenic carrier separation efficiency of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were improved. Hence CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) produced hydroxyl radicals more efficiently. Within 1 h, the degradation rate of MB by CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was about 99%. After five cycles of recycling, the degradation rate of MB by CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was still 92%. These results indicate that CQDs have good application potential in enhancing Fenton-like photodegradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MIL-88B(Fe)
Carbon quantum dots
Fenton-like photodegradation
Visible light
Hydroxyl radicals

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31971587 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Full XPS spectrum of CQDs, (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) FT-IR spectra of CQDs.
(a) PL spectrum of CQDs, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram of CQDs upon excitation at 300 nm, 340 nm, 380 nm, and 400 nm.

(a) (b) SEM images of MIL-88B(Fe), (c) CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), (d) TEM image of CQDs (inset shows the size distribution of CQDs), (e) TEM image of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), (f) HRTEM image of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) (inset shows a crystalline texture).
(a) (b) XRD patterns, (c) FT-IR spectra of photocatalysts.
XPS spectra of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe). (a) Survey scan, (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) Fe 2p of MIL-88B(Fe) and (e)Fe 2p of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe).
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, (b) pore size distribution of mesoporous, (c) pore size distribution of micropores.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectra, (b) Plots of (Ahν) 2 vs. photon energy (hν), (c) VB-XPS spectra.
PL spectra of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) under an excitation wavelength of 560 nm.
Photocurrent response signals of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe).

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB under visible light at pH = 5; (b) Photocatalytic degradation of MB over CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) at difffferent pH values; (c) comparison of photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts for MB removal via the addition of quenchers; (d) recycling experiments of photocatalysts.
Schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe).
Specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size.

head.ce:title: Wood-based carbon quantum dots for enhanced photocatalysis of MIL-88B(Fe)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Human Sport Science, Harbin Sport University, Harbin, 150001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vietnam National University of Forestry, Hanoi, 156220, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vietnam National University of Forestry, Hanoi 156220, Viet Nnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve the Fenton-like photodegradation efficiency of MIL-88B(Fe) in visible light, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared from poplar wood powder as a raw material and added to a precursor solution of MIL-88B(Fe) to prepare CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) materials using hydrothermal method. CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Brunner Emmett Teller (BET), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Ultraviolet visible light spectrophotometry (UV–vis), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectra (PL), and photocurrent test. In this study, methylene blue (MB) was used as the target pollutant to test the catalytic performance of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), and the main free radicals involved in the reaction were determined by adding superoxide dismutase (SOD) and isopropanol (IPA) for comparative experiments. The experimental results show that because CQDs can be used as a photosensitizer and electron trapping agent, the light absorption capacity and photogenic carrier separation efficiency of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were improved. Hence CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) produced hydroxyl radicals more efficiently. Within 1 h, the degradation rate of MB by CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was about 99%. After five cycles of recycling, the degradation rate of MB by CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was still 92%. These results indicate that CQDs have good application potential in enhancing Fenton-like photodegradation.

ce:section.ce:para: The dye pollution problem is endangering human water safety. Therefore, how to effectively deal with organic dyes in water has caused widespread concern. Fenton-like degradation can be used for treating pollutants in water, i.e., the use of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to generate strong oxidizing free radicals, thereby degrading the target pollutants using advanced oxidation technology [1–3]. In recent years, more and more Fenton-like photocatalysts have been studied [4–7]. MIL-88B (Fe) is an iron-based metal organic framework (MOF) with an appropriate band gap (approximately 2.0–2.3 eV) for photocatalysis [8,9]. Moreover, MIL-88B (Fe) is rich in open channels and has a larger specific surface area than traditional photocatalysts [10,11]. Under visible-light irradiation, an organic ligand can efficiently absorb solar energy and then transfer the energy to the active site. These active sites can contact pollutants in large areas through pores, and at the same time activate H2O2 to generate free radicals to degrade them [12,13]. Therefore, MIL-88B(Fe) is a very promising heterogeneous Fenton-like photocatalyst (see Table 1 ).

Although MIL-88B(Fe) has many advantages, its photogenerated carrier recombination rate is high, and the solar energy conversion efficiency under visible light is low, restricting the catalytic performance of MIL-88B(Fe) [14]. Mixing with carbon materials is one of the effective methods for improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Hang Zhang combined carbon nanotubes with MIL-88B(Fe) to improve the photocatalytic efficiency [15]. Tuan A. Vu prepared a composite of graphene oxide and MIL-88B(Fe) and improved the degradation effect of pollutants using the composite [16]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an emerging zero-dimensional carbon-based material. Wood powder, a processing waste, is a good carbon source for CQDs. Compared with other carbon materials, wood-based CQDs have diverse raw material sources, low cost, excellent optical properties, and outstanding electron-inducing ability [17,18]. The optical properties of CQDs could be further improved by N-doping, so that CQDs could be used in photocatalysis [20,26]. When combined with other materials, the light absorption capacity of a composite is enhanced. Furthermore, CQDs can be used as electron donors or electron acceptors, so that the composite has good light-induced electron transfer performance [19].

To the best of our knowledge, CQDs have been rarely used to promote Fenton-like reaction of MIL-88B(Fe). Therefore, in this study, CQDs were prepared using hydrothermal method with wood powder as a raw material, and CQDs were added to a precursor solution of MIL-88B(Fe) to successfully prepare CQDs@ MIL-88B(Fe) photocatalyst. The Fenton-like degradation performances of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) on methylene blue (MB) under visible light were compared. The results show that the photocatalytic performance of MIL-88B(Fe) loaded with CQDs considerably improved. Various experimental methods were used to study the photochemical properties and photocatalytic principles of these photocatalysts in detail.

Poplar wood flour was used as the raw material for CQDs. The reagents, including ethylenediamine (en), citric acid (CA), ferric chloride (FeCl3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), terephthalic acid (PTA), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), methyl alcohol, methylene blue (MB), superoxide dismutase (SQD), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were purchased from Tianjin Kermel chemical reagent limited company and used as received without further purification.

CQDs were produced using hydrothermal method. 2 g of poplar wood flour, 1 g of CA, and 200 μL of en were mixed in a beaker filled with 50 mL of deionized water. The mixture was sonicated for 20 min and stirred for 30 min to form a homogeneous suspension. Then, the suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 8 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the product was centrifuged to remove the solid residue. The residual solution was transferred to a 1000-Da dialysis bag for 48 h, and the deionized water was changed every 6 h to obtain a clear brown solution. The solution was then transferred to a vacuum freeze-dryer for drying for 24 h, and the remaining solids were collected for later use.

MIL-88B(Fe) was produced using a hydrothermal process. 1.62 g of FeCl3 and 1.67 g of PTA were dissolved in 50 mL of DMF. 4 mL (2 mol/L) of NaOH solution was slowly added to the mixture under stirring. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon liner reaction kettle and heated at 100 °C for 12 h. After the reaction kettle was naturally cooled to room temperature, the product was centrifuged. The orange product was washed three times with DMF, methanol, and deionized water, and dried at 110 °C for 12 h.

CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were prepared using one-step hydrothermal method: 0.5 g CQD solids were added into the precursor solution of MIL-88B(Fe). The remaining steps are the same as the preparation steps of MIL-88B(Fe).

The morphology of samples was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI SU8220). The microstructures of photocatalyst were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). The functional groups and their environment were observed by Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR, Nicolet 380) in an operating frequency range of 500–4000 cm−1. The crystal structure of samples was obtained using a X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker, D8 ADVANCE) with an operating energy of 40 KV/30 mA. The chemical composition and valence state of each element were analyzed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Escalab 250 Xi). The charge separation behavior and efficiency of prepared samples were determined using a photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer (FLS 980). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm was measured to determine the specific surface area and pore size distribution of samples (BET, TriStar II 3020). A UV spectrophotometer (UV–vis, UV-3600) was used to obtain the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra in the 200–900 nm spectral range and the UV–visible absorption spectra in the 200–800 nm spectral range. Moreover, the diffuse reflectance spectra were calibrated using the BaSO4 standard. Photocurrent was measured using a three-electrode electro-chemical system (CHI-660E, China).

The photocatalytic properties of samples were characterized by a decrease in the intensity of MB dye under visible light. 100 mL (10 mg/L) MB solution was added with 0.1 g CQDS@MIL-88B(Fe) catalyst sample, and the mixture was stirred under dark conditions for 12 h to achieve the adsorption equilibrium. Under the agitation condition, a 450-W xenon lamp was used as the light source to illuminate the dye degradation system, and a 420-nm filter was used to eliminate the UV radiation between the light source and dye degradation system. Samples were taken out every 10 min for 1 h. The sample was centrifuged to remove the residual photocatalyst, and the absorbance of supernatant at 664 nm was measured using a UV spectrophotometer, and the concentration of MB was calculated to evaluate the effect and efficiency of photocatalytic degradation. The degradation efficiency of MB was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1) Degradation (%) = [(C* – C)/C*] × 100
Where C* and C represent the initial concentration of MB after the adsorption-desorption equilibrium and the concentration of MB at an interval of 10 min, respectively [20].

The chemical composition of CQDs was analyzed by XPS. Strong peaks were observed corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s (Fig. 1 a). The high-resolution C 1s and N 1s spectra indicate the existence of various chemical bonds on the surface of CQDs. The C 1s spectrum can be distinguished into three independent peaks: 287.7 eV (CO), 286.0 eV (C–O/C–N), and 284.5 eV (C–C/CC), while the spectrum of N 1s can be distinguished into two peaks: 400.4 eV (pyrrolic N) and 399.5 eV (C–N–C) (Fig. 1b and c) [21–25]. This shows that the CQDs contain typical oxygen-containing functional groups, and the N element was successfully doped. The FT-IR spectrum shows the types of functional groups present on the surface of CQDs (Fig. 1d). A wide absorption peak at 3200–3400 cm−1 can be attributed to the absorption peak of –OH/−NH [25,28]. Peaks at 2926 cm−1 and 2853 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of = C–H [27]. A peak at 1649 cm−1 can be attributed to CO stretching vibration [20]. A peak at 1287 cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristics of aromatic C–NH stretching vibrations [29]. Peaks at 1180 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C–N [20]. The stretching vibration peak of C–OH appeared at 1048 cm−1 [20]. The presence of functional groups on the surface of quantum dots makes the quantum dots more water-soluble. The oxygen-containing functional groups present on the surface of CQDs make it easy for CQDs to combine with MIL−88B (Fe), laying a foundation for the preparation of composite materials [26].

The PL emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths show that the emission wavelength varies with the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 a). When the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 400 nm, the emission wavelength shifted from 400 nm to 460 nm, consistent with the results reported in previous literature [18]. This excitation-dependent PL behavior is probably caused by the surface defects and particle sizes of CQDs [21,24]. Fig. 2b shows the coordinates of CQDs at excitation wavelengths of 300 nm, 340 nm, 380 nm, and 400 nm in the CIE (Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage 1931 chromaticity) chromaticity diagram. The above results show that the CQDs prepared in this study has excellent optical properties [30].

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of photocatalysts. MIL-88B(Fe) has a typical biprism structure (Fig. 3a and b), consistent with previous reports in the literature [12,14]. CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) particles retain the same double prismatic structure as shown in Fig. 3c, and the particle length is about 1 μm. The morphological analysis further confirms the microstructure of CQDs and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) from TEM and high resolution (HR)TEM images: The uniformly distributed round dark patches in the figure are CQDs as shown in Fig. 3d. 100 carbon quantum dot particles were selected to measure the particle size, and the average value was taken. The average particle size was 7.75 nm (as shown in the inset of Fig. 3d). The double prismatic structure of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) can be seen more clearly, and CQDs were observed on the surface of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) in Fig. 3e and f. In the inset of Fig. 3f, a lattice spacing of 0.31 nm corresponds to the {002} crystal plane of graphite.

Fig. 4 a and b shows the X-ray diffraction pattern of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) powder. Diffraction peaks from 5° to 20° can be observed. The characteristic diffraction peaks of MIL-88B(Fe) at 9.5° and 10.8° are consistent with previous literature, indicating that MIL-88B(Fe) was successfully synthesized [15,32]. These two peaks are also detected in CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), indicating that the crystal structure of MIL-88B(Fe) did not change. Compared with MIL-88B(Fe), a decrease in the peak intensity in the XRD pattern of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) indicates that the periodicity of lattice decreases. This is because CQDs, a heterogeneous material, reduces the periodicity of lattice [33]. The relative intensity of diffraction peaks at 9.5° and 10.8° of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) changed, indicating that the doping of CQDs might have changed the dominant crystal surface exposed to CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe). No characteristic peak of CQDs was observed in the XRD pattern. This is probably because of a very small particle size and small loading of CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 4c, the surface groups of prepared samples were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. A wide absorption peak at ~3200 cm−1 can be attributed to the absorption peak of –OH/−NH [25,28]. Peaks at 2930 cm−1 and 2857 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of = C–H [27]. Peaks at 1685 cm−1 can be assigned to free carboxylic groups [34]. Peaks at 1385 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration, respectively [32]. The peak at 1503 cm−1 can be attributed to C–O asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations [35]. The peaks of Fe–O stretching vibration in iron-oxo clusters appeared at 749 cm−1 and 551 cm−1 [14]. A new absorption peak appeared at 928 cm−1, belonging to the stretching vibration peak of C–O–C in the ester group. This indicates that a small quantity of CQDs were crosslinked by esterification reaction between the surface –OH and –COOH groups in the framework. This increases the proportion of C–O–C groups and enhances the absorption peak. Thus, it can be concluded that CQDs were successfully loaded in the organic framework.

The surface element composition of photocatalysts was characterized via XPS. Fig. 5 a shows the full-scale XPS spectra of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe): Five peaks corresponding to Fe 2p1/2, Fe 2p3/2, O1s, N1s, and C1s were observed on the curve of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe). N1s was observed on the curve of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), indicating that CQDs were successfully introduced. Fig. 5b shows the high-resolution C1s spectrum, which can be divided into three peaks centered at 284.5 eV, 285.5 eV, and 288.5 eV. The peak centered at 284.5 eV corresponds to the C–N bond [32]. The peak at 285.5 eV can be attributed to the C–O bond. The binding energy of 288.5 eV can be assigned to the carboxylate functional group (O–CO) of H2BDC ligand [14,36]. Fig. 5c shows the high-resolution O1s spectrum, which was divided into three peaks centered at 529.9 eV, 531.5 eV, and 532.6 eV. The peak at 529.9 eV corresponds to the Fe–O bond in MIL-88B(Fe), and the peak at 531.5 eV corresponds to the Fe–O–C species. The peak at 532.6 eV corresponds to the oxygen vacancy, respectively [14,16]. Fig. 5d and e shows the high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum: The peaks of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 appeared at 724.7 eV and 711.2 eV, respectively [16]. After Gaussian curve fitting, the peaks at 711.1 eV, 712.8 eV, 724.7 eV, and 726.7 eV of MIL-88B(Fe) belong to Fe3+, and the peaks at 716.5 eV were the satellite peak, indicating that all iron elements in MIL-88B(Fe) exist in the form of Fe3+. Noticeably, in CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), in addition to all the above peaks, a peak belonging to Fe2+ appeared at 709.4 eV. According to calculations, the content of Fe2+ was about 6%.This is probably because the oxygen-rich functional groups on the CQD surface were selectively coordinated with MIL-88B(Fe).The electrons of CQDs oxygen-containing functional group can be transferred to MIL-88B(Fe), resulting in the production of Fe2+ in the catalyst. A small amount of Fe2+could also accelerate the transformation from Fe3+ to Fe2+ in the photocatalytic process, thus improving the photocatalytic rate [15,34]. The characteristic peaks of Fe3+ exhibited slight negative shifts of 0.2 eV relative to MIL-88B(Fe) (711.4 eV and 724.9 eV, respectively), while the binding energies of C1s and O1s in CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were 0.1 eV higher than those of MIL-88B(Fe). This can be attributed to the increased electron density on Fe and the decreased electron density on C and O, making it easier to lose 2P orbital electrons and thus reducing its binding energy [37].

The specific surface area, pore diameter distribution, and pore volume of synthesized catalyst were obtained from nitrogen adsorption desorption isotherms. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) in Fig. 6 a is consistent with Ⅳ type isotherm characteristics. This shows the mesoporous features in their microstructures, indicating that capillary condensation occurred during the adsorption of nitrogen [14,38]. The hysteresis loops were both H3 type, corresponding to the slit-shaped hole formed by the loose accumulation of particles [39]. MIL-88B(Fe) has a specific surface area of 118.25 m2g−1 and an average aperture of 3.49 nm, while the specific surface area and average pore diameter of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were 99.83 m2g−1 and 5.26 nm. The pore size distribution derived from the desorption isotherm of two samples (Fig. 6b and c) shows a range from 1 nm to 50 nm, especially concentrated at 2–5 nm. These data indicate that MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were both typical mesoporous materials and have a higher specific surface area, important for photocatalysis.

The TG curve of MIL-88B(Fe) in Fig. 7 explains the thermal stability of sample in air. The first mass loss from 27 °C to 150 °C can be attributed to adsorbed and coordinated water and DMF molecules. The mass loss from 150 °C to 360 °C was about 13%, caused by the removal of solvent molecules captured in the material hole. The mass loss from 360 °C to 760 °C was about 40%, mainly due to the decomposition of terephthalic acid in the skeleton of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe). The mass loss between 760 °C and 800 °C was about 8%, caused by the rapid release of CO2 due to the decomposition of carboxylate in the material. Finally, the residual mass of residual iron oxide was about 32%, consistent with the previous reports in the literature. This result indicates that CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) has a certain thermal stability [40].

The UV–vis spectra in Fig. 8 a show that MIL-88B(Fe) can absorb light from 200 nm to 630 nm, and the absorption curve of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) is similar to that of MIL-88B(Fe). However, the absorption cut-off edge could be extended to about 750 nm, indicating that the absorption capacity of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was stronger than that of MIL-88B(Fe) in the visible light range. Generally, the strong light absorption capacity of catalyst promoted a faster progress of photocatalysis [41,42]. The bandgap energies of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were calculated using “Tauc plot” method. The relationship between absorptivity (α) and photon energy (hv) can be expressed as (αhν)2 = A (hv−Eg). Because absorption (A) is proportional to absorptivity (α), the formula can be translated into (Ahν)2 = A (hv−Eg). By taking hv as the x-coordinate and (Ahν)2 as the y-coordinate, the bandgap energy of the two samples can be obtained from the tangent line in the linear part [20].As shown in Fig. 8b, the bandgap energy of MIL-88B(Fe) is about 2.04 eV, consistent with previously reported results. The bandgap of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) is 1.98 eV. The results show that the load of CQDs effectively increased the light absorption range of MIL-88B(Fe) and modification with CQDs resulted in a narrower band gap of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) Generally speaking, a narrow band gap is conducive to the generation of photogenerated carriers [43].

To determine the band structure of photocatalyst, the valence band (VB) spectra of XPS were used to evaluate the VB. As shown in Fig. 8c, the VB determined by XPS spectra was observed at 1.96 eV for MIL-88B(Fe) and 1.68 eV for CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe). The VB potential can be calculated using Eq. (2):
(2) EVB = ECB + Eg
Where ECB is the conduction band (CB) energy and Eg is the band gap [14,38]. The CB potentials of MIL-88B(Fe) and CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) were calculated as −0.14 eV and −0.29 eV, respectively.

PL spectra was measured to evaluate the photophysical properties of samples (Fig. 9 ). The composition of electrons and holes was qualitatively determined from fluorescence intensity. Generally, the lower the fluorescence intensity of the material, it was found that the lower the composite strength of photoluminescent carrier of the material in the PL spectrum, indicating faster separation and transfer of photogenerated electron (e−)—hole (h+) pairs [26,38]. The PL spectra under an excitation wavelength of 560 nm showed that the two samples had a strong fluorescence emission peak at 710 nm, and the emission peak intensity of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was significantly lower than that of MIL-88B(Fe), indicating that the introduction of CQDs inhibited the combination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the samples. A decrease in charge recombination rate has positive significance for photocatalysis.

To further explore the electronic properties of materials, photocurrent response measurements were carried out under visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). Fig. 10 shows that the intensity of photogenic current of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) is significantly higher than that of MIL-88B(Fe), indicating that the photogenic carrier separation efficiency of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) is much higher [14]. Because CQD captured photogenerated electrons, the transport of electron-hole pairs on CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was greatly promoted, consistent with the test results of PL spectroscopy [44].

The catalytic performance of photocatalysts was analyzed via the degradation of MB. As shown in Fig. 11 a, after dark reaction for 12 h, MIL-88B(Fe) absorbed 51% MB, while CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) absorbed only 22% MB. It is speculated that the introduction of CQDs blocked a part of the channel of MIL-88B(Fe) and reduced its specific surface area. When H2O2 was not added, the removal rate of MB by MIL-88B(Fe) was 60% within 1 h while the removal rate of MB by CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was 30%. The two groups of experiments with H2O2 added showed that the removal rate of MIL-88B(Fe) for MB within 1 h was about 84%. The removal rate of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) for MB was 85% in 10 min and 98% in 1 h. It is also clear from the slope of 0–10 min graph that the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) is much higher than that of MIL-88B(Fe). The reusability of catalyst is also an important index of catalyst performance. As shown in Fig. 11c, after five cycles, the removal rate of MB by CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) is 92%, indicating that CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) has high stability and can be reused.

pH is an important factor affecting the performance of catalyst, so we explored the photocatalytic performance of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) under different pH. Fig. 11b shows that the degradation rate of CQDs@MIL-88B (Fe) on MB in acidic environment is higher than that in neutral and alkaline environments. When pH is 2, CQDs@MIL-88B (Fe) exhibited the best photocatalytic performance. This can be attributed to the low oxidation potential of ·OH and the partial decomposition of H2O2 into O2 and H2O at a higher pH [45].

To determine the main groups involved in the reaction, isopropanol (IPA) and superoxide dismutase (SOD) were added into the system as the control group. SOD is an inhibitor of superoxide radical (·O2 −), and IPA is an inhibitor of hydroxyl radical (·OH). Fig. 11c shows that only a small amount of MB was degraded after the dark reaction of reaction system when IPA was added, while the degradation of MB in the reaction system with SOD was hardly affected. Therefore, it can be determined that the main group involved in the reaction is hydroxyl radical, and then the reaction mechanism of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) degradation of MB can be speculated.

Based on the characterization, the mechanism of photodegradation of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) can be elucidated. The UV–Vis spectra and (Ahν)2 vs. Photon Energy curves show that the introduction of carbon dots increases the absorption of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) to visible light and decreases the band gap of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), which is beneficial for the generation of photoelectric charge in the photocatalytic process. According to XPS data, during the preparation of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), the introduction of carbon quantum dots converts a part of Fe3+ into Fe2+. The existence of this part of Fe2+ helps to accelerate the production of ·OH and the cycle between Fe3+ and Fe2+, thereby accelerating the degradation of MB [15]. Notably, in the Fenton and Fenton-like system, H2O2 can capture photogenerated electrons to produce ·OH [46–48]. On the grounds of the fluorescence emission spectra and photocurrent spectrum, the introduction of carbon quantum dots significantly decreases the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers in CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe). Therefore, compared with MIL-88B(Fe), more electrons were captured by H2O2 in CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) to form more ·OH, promoting the degradation of MB. The schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) is shown in Fig. 12 .

Wood-based CQDs were successfully introduced into MIL-88B(Fe) by hydrothermal method. It was found that CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) utilize sunlight more efficiently and have a better band structure than pure MIL-88B(Fe) material. A lower recombination rate of photogenerated carriers makes CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) degrade MB faster, leading to a higher MB removal rate. In addition, the existence of a small number of Fe2+ also plays a role in the increase of optical system.

Huadong Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Xinchao Gong: Investigation, Formal analysis. Zihui Song: Investigation, Formal analysis. Shuo Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenxin Du: Investigation, Formal analysis. Tat Thang Nguyen: Data curation, Visualization. Minghui Guo: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Xing Gao: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31971587).
